executiveSummary: The Ranking View of Trade report suffers from severe performance degradation with queries consistently taking 130-150 seconds. The root cause is a combination of massive column over-selection (91 fact columns selected when only 8 are needed), date filtering via JOIN that prevents liquid clustering benefits on a 124.7 GB fact table, and pass-through serve views that expose all 96 columns. The PBI SPN generates 60,731 queries daily reading 60.47 TB total, with 2,682 queries exceeding 10 seconds. The #1 recommendation is to create narrow, report-specific serve views in dbt that select only the columns needed by each report and apply date filtering directly — this alone could reduce query time from 150s to under 10s. Health Score: 22/100 (Critical).

gitContext.description: dbt repo (asos-data-ade-dbt) and PBI repo (asos-data-ade-powerbi) both on latest main as of 2026-04-09. All 144 serve models are materialised as views. The fact_product_option_trade_daily_snapshot_v1 serve view selects all 96 columns from the curated Delta table with no filtering. No aggregation tables exist in the model.

topFindings.title: Column Over-Selection: 91 Columns Selected, Only 22 Needed
topFindings.description: Power BI DirectQuery generates SQL that selects ALL 91 columns from the daily trade fact table, plus ALL 47 columns from dim_date_v2 and ALL 75 columns from dim_product_option_v1. The actual Ranking View visual only needs 8 aggregate values and 14 grouping columns. This forces Databricks to read and transmit 4-5x more data than necessary, directly increasing I/O time and network transfer.
topFindings.recommendation:
**Engineering**: Create a narrow serve view `serve_fact_trade_ranking_v1` in dbt that selects only the 22 columns used by the Ranking View report: dim_date_sk, dim_product_option_sk, retail_sale_buy_value, retail_sale_value, retail_sale_quantity, retail_sale_total_realised_markdown, view_count, view_count_weighted_by_available_sizes, view_count_of_available_sizes, number_of_weeks_on_site, plus the foreign key columns for active relationships. File: `bundles/core_data/models/sales/serve/serve_fact_trade_ranking_v1.sql`.

**Semantic Model**: In the ADE - Trade model, change the `Daily Trade` table partition to point to the new narrow view instead of the full `fact_product_option_trade_daily_snapshot_v1`. Alternatively, hide all unused columns in Tabular Editor (though DirectQuery will still request them internally).
topFindings.estimatedImprovement: 60-70% reduction in read bytes (from 14.6 GB to ~4-5 GB per query). Query time reduction from 150s to ~50-60s from column pruning alone.
tradeoffs: Creating report-specific serve views adds maintenance overhead — each new report may need its own view
The narrow view must be kept in sync with model changes (new measures may need additional columns)
Hiding columns in Tabular Editor does NOT reduce DirectQuery payload — Databricks still reads them

options.name: Option A: Report-specific narrow serve view
options.description: Create a new dbt view selecting only needed columns
options.pros: Maximum column reduction, no PBI changes needed, can add date WHERE clause too
options.cons: Requires dbt deploy cycle, adds a new model to maintain

options.name: Option B: Hide unused columns in PBI model
options.description: Use Tabular Editor to hide 650+ unused columns
options.pros: No engineering work, immediate in PBI Desktop
options.cons: DirectQuery still selects hidden columns internally — no actual performance gain

options.name: Option C: Materialised narrow view in Databricks
options.description: Create a materialised Delta table with only needed columns and pre-filtered date range
options.pros: Eliminates view overhead, enables independent clustering optimisation
options.cons: Requires ETL scheduling, increases storage costs, data freshness depends on refresh frequency

topFindings.title: Date Filter via JOIN Prevents Liquid Clustering Benefits
topFindings.description: The query applies a 2-year date filter (2024-04-06 to 2026-04-06) to dim_date_v2 via WHERE, then JOINs to the fact table on dim_date_sk. The fact table is liquid clustered on dim_date_sk, but because the filter is on the dimension side of the JOIN, Databricks cannot use clustering statistics to skip irrelevant files. The query also narrows to 'Yesterday' via dim_financial_time_period_v1, but this further restriction also cannot be pushed to the fact table. Result: 1,236 files read vs 1,475 pruned — only 54% pruning efficiency when it should be 99%+ for a single day.
topFindings.recommendation:
**Engineering**: Add a direct `WHERE dim_date_sk >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL 2 YEAR` clause to the narrow serve view. For the 'Yesterday' pattern, create a filtered view `serve_fact_trade_daily_yesterday_v1` that includes `WHERE dim_date_sk = CURRENT_DATE - 1`. This pushes the date predicate directly to the Delta table scan.

**Semantic Model**: Restructure the model so that the `Daily Trade` table's M expression applies a date parameter filter. Change the partition expression from the pass-through `_fn_GetDataFromDBX(...)` to include an M-level date filter. Alternatively, add a calculated table or query parameter for the date range.
topFindings.estimatedImprovement: 90-95% reduction in rows read (from 2.7B to ~50-100M for a 2-year window, or ~5-10M for 'Yesterday' only). Query time from 150s to ~5-15s.
tradeoffs: M-level date filtering requires careful parameter management across environments (dev/test/prod)
A fixed 'Yesterday' view limits flexibility — users may want to select different time periods
Adding WHERE to the serve view changes the contract — all downstream consumers must tolerate filtered data

options.name: Option A: WHERE clause in serve view
options.description: Add date filter directly in dbt serve view SQL
options.pros: Simple, effective, Databricks pushes filter to Delta scan
options.cons: Breaks serve view contract if other consumers need full history

options.name: Option B: M-level date parameter in PBI
options.description: Modify _fn_GetDataFromDBX to accept date parameters and apply WHERE in M query
options.pros: Filter pushed at source, no dbt changes needed
options.cons: Complex M expression management, PBI Desktop refresh may behave differently

options.name: Option C: Create pre-filtered aggregation table
options.description: Materialise a rolling 2-year aggregation in dbt as a Delta table
options.pros: Eliminates scan entirely, sub-second queries possible
options.cons: Requires ETL pipeline, storage cost, and refresh scheduling

topFindings.title: Pass-Through Serve Views Expose 96 Columns with No Filtering
topFindings.description: All serve views for the trade domain (fact_product_option_trade_daily_snapshot_v1, fact_product_option_trade_weekly_snapshot_v1) are simple `SELECT * FROM curated_table` with no column narrowing and no pre-filtering. This means every DirectQuery hit scans the entire 124.7 GB curated table regardless of what the report actually needs. The daily snapshot serve view exposes 96 columns; the weekly exposes 94 columns.
topFindings.recommendation:
**Engineering**: Refactor serve views to select only the columns that are actually consumed by Power BI models. Audit column usage by cross-referencing `model-taxonomy.json` (which columns appear in relationships, measures, and visuals) with the serve view columns. For `serve_fact_product_option_trade_daily_snapshot_v1`, the ~22 actively used columns should be the SELECT list.

File to modify: `bundles/core_data/models/sales/serve/serve_fact_product_option_trade_daily_snapshot_v1.sql`

Change from:
```sql
SELECT * FROM {{ ref('curated_fact_product_option_trade_daily_snapshot_v1') }}
```
To:
```sql
SELECT dim_date_sk, dim_product_option_sk, dim_pricing_store_sk, ... (only needed columns)
FROM {{ ref('curated_fact_product_option_trade_daily_snapshot_v1') }}
```
topFindings.estimatedImprovement: 50-60% reduction in I/O per query. Combined with date filtering (F2), total improvement could reach 95%+.
tradeoffs: Narrowing the serve view affects ALL consumers of that view, not just the Ranking View report
If other PBI reports use columns not in the narrow view, they will break
Alternative: create a new narrow view and keep the existing one for backward compatibility

options.name: Option A: Narrow existing serve view
options.description: Remove unused columns from the current serve view
options.pros: Single change, all reports benefit immediately
options.cons: Breaking change for any consumer using the removed columns

options.name: Option B: Create parallel narrow view
options.description: Add a new serve_fact_trade_ranking_v1 alongside the existing view
options.pros: Non-breaking, targeted optimisation
options.cons: Additional model to maintain

topFindings.title: No Aggregation Tables for 124.7 GB DirectQuery Fact Table
topFindings.description: The ADE - Trade model uses DirectQuery against a 124.7 GB fact table with no aggregation tables defined. The BPA rule MODEL_USING_DIRECT_QUERY_AND_NO_AGGREGATIONS is explicitly ignored in the model configuration. Every visual interaction triggers a full scan of the curated Delta table. Pre-aggregated import tables could serve 80%+ of common queries from in-memory cache, falling back to DirectQuery only for drill-through or unusual filter combinations.
topFindings.recommendation:
**Engineering**: Create a pre-aggregated Delta table in dbt: `curated_agg_trade_daily_by_option_v1` that pre-computes SUM of all metric columns grouped by dim_date_sk, dim_product_option_sk, and key dimension foreign keys. Materialise it as an incremental table (daily refresh). Size estimate: ~500 MB (vs 124.7 GB).

**Semantic Model**: In Tabular Editor, add the aggregation table as an Import mode table with the same column names as the fact table. Configure aggregation mappings (SUM for metrics, GroupBy for dimensions). PBI will automatically route matching queries to the aggregation table.
topFindings.estimatedImprovement: 90-95% of queries served from import cache (sub-second). Only complex drill-through queries hit DirectQuery. Overall page load from 150s to <5s.
tradeoffs: Aggregation tables add ETL complexity and storage costs (~500 MB incremental)
Data freshness depends on refresh schedule (hourly/daily)
Aggregation mappings must be carefully configured — incorrect mappings produce wrong results silently
Requires PBI Premium or Fabric capacity for import + DQ hybrid mode

options.name: Option A: dbt aggregation table + PBI aggregation
options.description: Create pre-aggregated table in dbt, map in PBI as import agg table
options.pros: Most dramatic performance improvement, standard Microsoft pattern
options.cons: High effort, requires dbt + PBI + ETL coordination

options.name: Option B: Databricks materialised view
options.description: Use Databricks materialised view instead of dbt model
options.pros: Auto-refresh, no dbt pipeline needed
options.cons: Limited SQL support in materialised views, less control over refresh timing

options.name: Option C: Incremental Refresh in PBI
options.description: Configure Incremental Refresh on the fact table partition to import recent data
options.pros: No engineering work, PBI-native feature
options.cons: Requires Premium capacity, refresh time for 124 GB table may be prohibitive

topFindings.title: 3 Bidirectional Cross-Filter Relationships on DirectQuery Tables
topFindings.description: The model has 3 bidirectional cross-filter relationships. Bidirectional filtering on DirectQuery tables generates additional SQL queries and can cause ambiguous filter propagation, leading to incorrect results or excessive query fan-out. Each bidirectional relationship can double the number of DirectQuery round-trips for a single visual.
topFindings.recommendation:
**Semantic Model**: In Tabular Editor, change all 3 bidirectional relationships to single-direction (from dimension to fact). Audit each relationship to confirm the correct filter direction:
1. Open the model in Tabular Editor
2. Navigate to Model > Relationships
3. For each bidirectional relationship, set `CrossFilteringBehavior` to `OneDirection`
4. Test report visuals to confirm no functional regression

If a visual requires bidirectional filtering for a specific interaction, use `CROSSFILTER()` in the DAX measure instead of a model-level bidirectional relationship.
topFindings.estimatedImprovement: 10-20% reduction in DirectQuery round-trips. Eliminates ambiguous filter paths.
tradeoffs: Some visuals may lose filter interactivity if they rely on bidirectional propagation
Requires testing each affected visual to ensure correct behaviour
Using CROSSFILTER() in DAX is less performant than a model relationship but more targeted

options.name: Option A: Change to single-direction
options.description: Set all 3 relationships to OneDirection in Tabular Editor
options.pros: Simple, immediate effect, best practice
options.cons: May break visuals that rely on bidirectional filter propagation

options.name: Option B: Use CROSSFILTER() in specific measures
options.description: Remove bidirectional at model level, add CROSSFILTER() only where needed
options.pros: Targeted, no broad impact
options.cons: More complex DAX, harder to maintain

topFindings.title: 650 Unused Columns Inflating DirectQuery Payload
topFindings.description: BPA analysis identified 650 columns that are not referenced by any measure, relationship, or calculated column. In DirectQuery mode, PBI includes all visible columns in the generated SQL SELECT — even columns that no visual uses. This inflates the query payload and increases Databricks scan time.
topFindings.recommendation:
**Semantic Model**: Hide or remove the 650 unused columns in Tabular Editor:
1. Export the BPA results to identify all UNUSED_COLUMNS_CANDIDATE findings
2. In Tabular Editor, select each unused column and set `IsHidden = true`
3. For columns that are definitively not needed (not used in bookmarks, drillthrough, or external tools), delete them entirely from the model

**Important**: Hiding columns in DirectQuery does NOT prevent PBI from selecting them in SQL. To get actual performance benefit, columns must be removed from the model entirely (delete, not just hide). However, deletion is riskier — validate with report authors first.
topFindings.estimatedImprovement: If columns are deleted (not just hidden): 30-40% reduction in SELECT column count, proportional I/O reduction. If only hidden: no performance benefit, but improved model usability.
tradeoffs: Deleting columns is irreversible and may break bookmarks, drillthrough pages, or external tools
Hidden columns are still selected by DirectQuery — hiding alone provides no performance benefit
Column removal requires coordinating with all report authors who use this model

options.name: Option A: Hide unused columns
options.description: Set IsHidden=true on all 650 candidates
options.pros: Low risk, improves model usability
options.cons: No DirectQuery performance benefit

options.name: Option B: Delete unused columns
options.description: Remove columns entirely from the model
options.pros: Actual performance improvement in DirectQuery queries
options.cons: High risk, may break existing reports or bookmarks

options.name: Option C: Narrow serve view (preferred)
options.description: Create a narrow dbt view that only exposes needed columns (combines with F1)
options.pros: Performance benefit without model changes, backward compatible
options.cons: Requires dbt deploy

topFindings.title: 74 FILTER(ALL) Anti-Patterns in DAX Measures
topFindings.description: 74 DAX measures use FILTER(ALL(Table)) patterns which iterate over the full table in memory, bypassing any existing filter context. In DirectQuery mode, each FILTER(ALL) generates an additional SQL query that scans the entire table without filters. This is the most common DAX anti-pattern affecting DirectQuery performance.
topFindings.recommendation:
**Semantic Model**: Replace FILTER(ALL(Table), condition) with the equivalent KEEPFILTERS + CALCULATE pattern:

Before:
```dax
CALCULATE([Measure], FILTER(ALL(Table), Table[Column] = "Value"))
```
After:
```dax
CALCULATE([Measure], KEEPFILTERS(Table[Column] = "Value"))
```
Or if you need to remove existing filters:
```dax
CALCULATE([Measure], REMOVEFILTERS(Table[Column]), Table[Column] = "Value")
```

Prioritise the 10 measures flagged as top offenders in the DAX audit, as they have the most anti-patterns per measure.
topFindings.estimatedImprovement: 10-30% reduction in DirectQuery round-trips, depending on how many FILTER(ALL) measures are active on the current page. Most impactful for pages with many KPI cards.
tradeoffs: DAX refactoring requires deep understanding of the business logic to ensure equivalence
KEEPFILTERS behaves differently from FILTER(ALL) when combined with other filters — test thoroughly
74 measures is a large refactoring scope — prioritise by usage frequency and visual binding

options.name: Option A: Targeted refactoring
options.description: Refactor only the 10-15 measures used on the Ranking View page
options.pros: Focused effort, immediate impact on this report
options.cons: Other pages still affected

options.name: Option B: Bulk refactoring with regex
options.description: Use Tabular Editor Advanced Scripting to batch-replace FILTER(ALL) patterns
options.pros: Efficient, covers all 74 measures
options.cons: Regex replacement may miss edge cases — requires validation

topFindings.title: 62 Critical-Complexity Measures with 7x Nested CALCULATE
topFindings.description: 62 measures have critical complexity scores (15+), characterised by 72-line expressions, 7 levels of nested CALCULATE, SUMX iterators, and FILTER(ALL) patterns. These WTD (Week-to-Date) measures generate extremely complex SQL with multiple correlated subqueries, compounding the DirectQuery performance problem.
topFindings.recommendation:
**Semantic Model**: Refactor WTD measures to use a shared base pattern. Create a single `_WTD_Filter` calculated table or measure that encapsulates the week-to-date logic, then have each WTD measure reference it:

```dax
[Retail Sale Value WTD] = 
    CALCULATE(
        [Retail Sale Value],
        _WTD_DateFilter
    )
```

Where `_WTD_DateFilter` is a reusable variable or calculated table that contains the week-to-date date range. This reduces the 72-line pattern to 3-4 lines per measure and generates simpler SQL.
topFindings.estimatedImprovement: 15-25% improvement in query generation efficiency. Simpler SQL means faster Databricks compilation and potentially better query plan optimisation.
tradeoffs: Refactoring 62 measures is a significant effort requiring DAX expertise
Shared patterns may not cover all edge cases (different financial calendars, aggregation types)
Testing is critical — WTD calculations are business-critical and errors are highly visible

options.name: Option A: Shared WTD pattern
options.description: Create reusable WTD base measures and simplify all 62
options.pros: Dramatic code reduction, easier maintenance
options.cons: Large refactoring scope, requires deep DAX knowledge

options.name: Option B: Pre-compute WTD in dbt
options.description: Add WTD aggregations as columns in the curated/serve layer
options.pros: Eliminates complex DAX entirely, simple SUM in PBI
options.cons: Moves business logic to engineering layer, harder to change

topFindings.title: Cross-Catalog JOINs Across 3 Databricks Catalogs
topFindings.description: Each DirectQuery hit JOINs tables from sales.serve, technology.serve, and product.serve catalogs in a single SQL statement. Cross-catalog JOINs in Databricks require the query engine to coordinate across multiple storage locations, potentially adding network overhead and reducing parallelism.
topFindings.recommendation:
**Engineering**: Denormalise the most commonly joined dimensions into the fact table serve view. For the Ranking View, embed dim_product_option attributes (option_id, description, buyrarchy, prices) and dim_date attributes (calendar_date, financial_year_week_id) directly in the narrow serve view. This eliminates 2 of 3 cross-catalog JOINs.

File: `bundles/core_data/models/sales/serve/serve_fact_trade_ranking_v1.sql`
```sql
SELECT f.dim_date_sk, d.calendar_date, d.financial_year_week_id,
       p.option_id, p.option_description, p.product_buyrarchy_division,
       f.retail_sale_value, f.retail_sale_buy_value, ...
FROM {{ ref('curated_fact_product_option_trade_daily_snapshot_v1') }} f
JOIN technology.serve.dim_date_v2 d ON f.dim_date_sk = d.dim_date_sk
JOIN product.serve.dim_product_option_v1 p ON f.dim_product_option_sk = p.dim_product_option_sk
WHERE f.dim_date_sk >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL 2 YEAR
```
topFindings.estimatedImprovement: 10-15% query time reduction from eliminating cross-catalog coordination overhead.
tradeoffs: Denormalised views increase maintenance complexity and storage
Dimension changes require view updates (e.g., new product attributes)
May conflict with ASOS data governance policies on single-source-of-truth

options.name: Option A: Denormalised serve view
options.description: Pre-join dimensions into the serve view
options.pros: Eliminates cross-catalog JOINs entirely
options.cons: Data governance concern, increased view complexity

options.name: Option B: Keep cross-catalog, optimise elsewhere
options.description: Focus on F1-F3 first, re-evaluate cross-catalog impact after
options.pros: No architectural change needed
options.cons: Cross-catalog overhead remains

topFindings.title: 18 Report-Level Filters Creating Heavy Filter Propagation
topFindings.description: The Ranking View of Trade report has 18 report-level filters applied across all 12 visuals. Each report-level filter adds WHERE clauses to every DirectQuery visual query on the page. While individual filters are lightweight, 18 filters compound to create complex WHERE clauses that the Databricks optimiser may not handle efficiently.
topFindings.recommendation:
**Power BI**: Review the 18 report-level filters and assess which are actually needed:
1. Open the report in Power BI Desktop
2. In the Filters pane, review each report-level filter
3. Remove filters that are redundant (already applied at page or visual level)
4. Convert broad filters to slicers where user interaction is expected
5. For static filters (e.g., 'exclude internal orders'), consider applying them in the dbt serve view instead
topFindings.estimatedImprovement: 5-10% reduction in query complexity. Minimal performance impact but improves report maintainability.
tradeoffs: Removing filters may expose data that should be hidden
Converting to slicers changes the user experience
Moving filters to dbt removes user flexibility

options.name: Option A: Audit and remove redundant filters
options.description: Review each of the 18 filters and remove duplicates
options.pros: Quick, no engineering needed
options.cons: Requires understanding each filter's business purpose

options.name: Option B: Move static filters to serve view
options.description: Apply permanent filters (e.g., exclude wholesale) in dbt
options.pros: Reduces PBI query complexity permanently
options.cons: Requires dbt deploy, less flexible for users

actions.action: Create narrow serve view serve_fact_trade_ranking_v1 with only needed columns + date WHERE clause
Change 3 bidirectional relationships to single-direction in Tabular Editor
Audit and remove redundant report-level filters
Delete definitively unused columns from the semantic model (coordinate with report authors)
Refactor top 10 FILTER(ALL) measures used on Ranking View page
Update ADE - Trade model to point Daily Trade table to narrow serve view
Create pre-aggregated Delta table curated_agg_trade_daily_by_option_v1 in dbt
Configure PBI aggregation table mapping in Tabular Editor
Refactor all 62 critical-complexity WTD measures to shared base pattern
Evaluate denormalising key dimensions into serve view to eliminate cross-catalog JOINs
Bulk-refactor remaining 64 FILTER(ALL) measures across all pages
Implement Databricks query monitoring dashboard for PBI SPN queries
